sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 2 actions in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: SPACE, DOWN.
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 19, level 1.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, SPACE, MOUSE.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current knowledge ledger from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE LEDGER.
Pass it as the `world_model` argument of the `python` tool call, alongside `code`. That argument is the one that carries forward, so send it on every call, even when you reply with a tool call and no assistant text.
Game-tier fields (action_semantics, object_taxonomy, hud_map, win_pattern, level_log, cross_level_notes) persist across levels; level-tier fields (world_model, goal_model, recent_findings, open_questions, current_plan, strategy_log, failed_probes) reset when a level ends. Record every unproductive probe in failed_probes so it is never repeated, and tag claims VERIFIED(step N) or HYPOTHESIS.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
The `world_model` tool argument is the reliable way to update the ledger. Assistant text is optional; if you write any, keep it short and prefix lines with `Action semantics:`, `Object taxonomy:`, `HUD map:`, `Win pattern:`, `Level log:`, `Cross-level notes:`, `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, or `Failed probes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 18 of about 55 target actions so far.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Interesting! After SPACE, the center is back to id=12 (white, 84px) and id=13 (white, 100px). The frame didn't move with DOWN.
Wait, something is different from before. Earlier when I pressed SPACE the first time (from the initial state), id=13 changed from white to purple+white. But now, pressing SPACE again from the merged state (id=12 white, id=13 white), it stayed white-white. Let me check more carefully.
Actually, looking at the output: after SPACE, id=13 is white (100px). That means SPACE didn't split it this time. Or wait, maybe SPACE toggles and since we were already in the "split" state before (from the earlier SPACE), this SPACE merged back, and the next SPACE would split again.
Wait no, let me re-read. Before this SPACE, the state was: id=12 (white), id=13 (white, 100px) - two objects. After SPACE: still id=12 (white), id=13 (white, 100px) - two objects. So SPACE did nothing this time?
Hmm, but board_changed was True. Let me check what changed. The output shows id=13 is white with 100px, same as before. But the timer changed (id=14 boundary).
Wait, actually I need to be more careful. Let me look at the state before this SPACE call. The previous state was after the earlier SPACE which merged things: id=12 (white), id=13 (white, 100px). Then I called SPACE again. The result shows id=13 is still white with 100px. So SPACE didn't split this time.
But wait, the first time I pressed SPACE (step 0), the state went from id=13 (white, 100px) to id=13 (purple, 50px) + id=14 (white, 50px). The second time I pressed SPACE, it went back to id=13 (white, 100px). Now the third time, it stayed at id=13 (white, 100px).
So maybe SPACE only works once in each direction? Or maybe SPACE only splits when id=12 is purple? Let me check:
- First SPACE: id=12 was purple, id=13 was white -> split into purple+white
- Second SPACE: id=12 was white, id=13 was purple, id=14 was white -> merged to white
- Third SPACE: id=12 was white, id=13 was white -> no change (already merged)
So SPACE merges if there are 3 objects, splits if there are 2 objects. But the third time, there were already 2 objects and it didn't split. Maybe SPACE only splits once (the first time), and after merging, it can't split again?
Or maybe the split behavior depends on id=12's color. First time id=12 was purple, so it split. Now id=12 is white, so it doesn't split.
Let me try: make id=12 purple, then SPACE. If it splits, that confirms the hypothesis.
Actually wait, I need to reconsider. Maybe SPACE doesn't toggle at all. Maybe:
- SPACE when id=12 is purple: splits id=13
- SPACE when id=12 is white: does nothing (or merges)
Let me try making id=12 purple and then pressing SPACE.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Make id=12 purple, then SPACE
action([{'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 4, 'col': 43}])  # purple indicator
print(f"After painting purple:")
seg = current_frame.segmentation
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['id'] in [12, 13]:
        b = n['boundary']
        print(f"  id={n['id']}: color={n['color']} px={n['pixels']}")

# Now SPACE
action(['SPACE'])
print(f"\nAfter SPACE:")
seg2 = current_frame.segmentation
for n in seg2['nodes']:
    if n['id'] in [11, 12, 13, 14]:
        b = n['boundary']
        print(f"  id={n['id']}: color={n['color']} px={n['pixels']} [{b[0]}]-[{b[2] if len(b)>2 else b[0]}]")
print(f"Level completed: {last_action_result.get('level_completed')}")
</parameter>
<parameter=world_model>
{"game_tier": {"action_semantics": {"SPACE": "Splits id=13 when id=12 is purple; merges when 3 objects exist", "UP/DOWN/LEFT/RIGHT": "Tick down timer only", "MOUSE on top-right indicator": "Paints id=12 with that color"}, "object_taxonomy": {"id_11": "Gray frame around id=12", "id_12": "Top center object (paintable)", "id_13": "Bottom center object(s)"}, "hud_map": {"bottom_strip": "Timer bar", "top_right": "Color palette"}, "win_pattern": {}, "level_log": {}, "cross_level_notes": ""}, "level_tier": {"world_model": "SPACE splits id=13 only when id=12 is purple. Merge takes bottom color. Current: id=12(white), id=13(white,100px).", "goal_model": "Match center to left panel: white above purple", "recent_findings": ["SPACE didn't split when id=12 was white", "Hypothesis: SPACE only splits when id=12 is purple"], "open_questions": ["Does SPACE split when id=12 is purple?"], "current_plan": "Paint id=12 purple, then SPACE to test", "strategy_log": [], "failed_probes": ["SPACE when id=12 white: no split"]}}
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

sections.content:

  id=12: color=p px=84

  id=12: color=p px=84 [[25, 26]]-[[31, 37]]
  id=13: color=p px=50 [[34, 27]]-[[38, 36]]
